title: Lifecycle, Storage Classes, Archive Restore, and Cost · Execution Trace: Retention Policy Conflict
summary: A policy transitions active content immediately, expires current versions but not hidden history, and promises instant archive access without retrieval or minimum-duration cost. Predict the representation, storage, trust, cost, and recovery evidence before inspecting a model. Planned artifact: Add and verify the retention policy conflict increment in lifecycle and storage-cost decision table. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evi…
objectives: Resolve lifecycle expiration against versioning, Object Lock, legal hold, replication, business retention, and deletion evidence.
Request, monitor, authorize, and communicate temporary archive restoration with tier, expiry, duplicate request, and failure states.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and Go Predict Retention Policy Conflict · Theory Retention Policy Conflict
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory Go file-delivery team handles case 8225 during a private media release. Build deterministic Go evidence to evaluate lifecycle transition, retention lock, restore, and minimum-duration cost; preserve accessible progress, cancellation, status, and recovery evidence; then change region, range, or signed header and explain the exact failure boundary. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, databases, credentials, host state, race and leak checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Resolve lifecycle expiration against versioning, Object Lock, legal hold, replication, business retention, and deletion evidence.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Retention Policy Conflict contract for the supplied data.
